topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp white paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips as I unfolded the document. Every carefully curated achievement, every whispered anecdote about my childhood—it was all there, meticulously crafted, and none of it was mine. A slow, cold burn started low in my stomach, spreading outward until my jaw was clenched so tight I could feel the muscle tremor. I reread the passage detailing my 'defining moment,' the description so perfectly tailored to the narrative they’d spun. A sharp, almost painful clarity cut through the haze of disbelief. I didn't cry. Instead, I smoothed the paper flat on the mahogany desk, my movements precise, almost surgical. The air in the room suddenly felt thin, charged with a simmering, focused energy. I started making calls, my voice low and utterly steady, each word measured, each inquiry a carefully placed stone. They would learn exactly what it felt like to have their foundation dismantled.

[story 2]

I stared at the typeface, the elegant script mocking me with its flawless execution. For months, I’d accepted the accolades, the carefully constructed life, but this—this theft of my own story—was an intolerable affront. A deep, unsettling heat rose in my chest, pushing against my ribs. I didn't waste time on tears. Instead, I walked to the window, watching the city lights blur below, feeling a profound sense of righteous anger settle over me, heavy and solid. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the contact list of the individuals involved. A slow, deliberate sequence of actions began: researching old correspondence, cross-referencing dates, gathering every scrap of verifiable information. There was a quiet satisfaction in the methodical dismantling of their carefully built façade. I imagined the look on their faces when the pieces started to fall into place, and that image fueled a sharp, almost predatory focus in my eyes.

[story 3]

When I finally understood the scope of the deception, the world seemed to tilt on its axis. The realization wasn't sudden; it was a creeping poison, saturating every thought. A slow, deliberate stillness took over my posture. My hands, resting on the cool surface of the table, curled into tight fists. Beneath the surface, a fierce, undeniable energy was building, a pressurized force demanding release. I didn't need to shout. I needed to correct the record, piece by agonizing piece. I began drafting letters, not angry rants, but meticulously organized indictments. Each sentence was chosen with precision, designed to expose the gaps, the fabrications. A grim smile touched my lips—a tight, unpleasant expression that didn't reach my eyes. It was the look of someone who has found the exact leverage needed to bring down a structure they know is rotten, and they were eager to start the demolition.
